original_hypothesis.title: TwoWay LockedStatus Enum Defined But Functionally Identical to OneWay - Missing Reverse Functionality
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: The IMerge interface defines three LockedStatus values: Locked, OneWay, and TwoWay. However, the onTokenTransfer function only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`, treating OneWay and TwoWay identically. No code path exists that differentiates TwoWay from OneWay. If TwoWay was intended to enable reversible deposits (returning TGT to depositors in exchange for TITN), this functionality is completely missing. Users or governance processes expecting a TwoWay mode to provide deposit reversal have no recourse.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The enum explicitly names a TwoWay state suggesting bidirectional exchange was planned. The absence of any differentiating code means this is either dead code or missing functionality.

proof_of_signal.pattern: onTokenTransfer only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`; no code anywhere differentiates TwoWay from OneWay; no reverse exchange function exists

preconditions: Users expect TwoWay to enable deposit reversal

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: TwoWay enum value exists but has zero functional differentiation from OneWay
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Owner can set TwoWay but it has no unique effect

operational_questions: Was TwoWay intended to enable reverse exchanges (TITN → TGT)?
Are users or documentation referencing TwoWay functionality?
Is there a planned upgrade to implement TwoWay?
validation_requirements: Confirm no code path differentiates TwoWay from OneWay
Search for any reverse exchange function in the contract

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Users deposit TGT expecting TwoWay mode to allow reversal
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: No reversal function exists - TGT is permanently locked
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_3: Users are misled by the TwoWay status into irrevocable deposits
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_4: Only owner withdraw() can return TGT

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Users may irreversibly deposit TGT under false TwoWay expectations
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Only owner can return TGT via withdraw()

validation_result.impact_summary: The TwoWay enum value is defined in IMerge.sol but has zero functional differentiation from OneWay in any code path. If owner sets TwoWay, deposits proceed identically to OneWay with no reverse exchange capability, representing dead code and potentially missing functionality.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The IMerge interface defines three LockedStatus values at IMerge.sol lines 5-9: Locked (0), OneWay (1), and TwoWay (2). In MergeTgt.sol, the onTokenTransfer function at lines 73-74 only checks `lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked`, meaning both OneWay and TwoWay allow deposits identically. A comprehensive search of the entire MergeTgt.sol contract reveals no code path that checks for LockedStatus.TwoWay specifically, no reverse exchange function (TITN→TGT), and no differentiated behavior of any kind. The setLockedStatus function at line 149 accepts any LockedStatus value including TwoWay but the value has no unique effect. The deployment script arbitrumSetup.ts uses `setLockedStatus(1)` which is OneWay, confirming TwoWay is not used in actual deployment. This constitutes dead code (the TwoWay enum value serves no purpose) and potentially missing functionality (bidirectional exchange implied by the name is never implemented).
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: IMerge.sol lines 5-9 define the enum with three values. MergeTgt.sol lines 73-74 show the only check against lockedStatus is `== LockedStatus.Locked`. No other location in MergeTgt.sol references LockedStatus.TwoWay or LockedStatus.OneWay specifically. The entire contract has no function that converts TITN back to TGT. This is conclusive evidence that TwoWay is dead code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: TwoWay enum value is defined with semantic meaning (bidirectional exchange) but no corresponding functionality exists anywhere in the codebase. This is dead code.
Only checks for Locked status. Both OneWay and TwoWay pass this check identically. No code differentiates TwoWay from OneWay.
Accepts TwoWay as a valid status but setting it produces no different behavior from OneWay. Owner and users could be misled about available functionality.

calling_sequence: 1. Owner calls setLockedStatus(LockedStatus.TwoWay) - lockedStatus set to TwoWay
2. User calls TGT.transferAndCall(MergeTgt, amount) which triggers onTokenTransfer
3. onTokenTransfer checks lockedStatus == LockedStatus.Locked (false for TwoWay), deposit proceeds
4. Deposit behaves identically to OneWay mode - no reverse exchange capability exists
5. User's TGT is permanently deposited with no TITN→TGT reversal path
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The entire contract lacks any function that performs a reverse exchange (TITN→TGT). Searching all external/public functions: constructor, deposit, withdraw (owner-only, any token), onTokenTransfer (TGT→TITN only), tgtBalance, titnBalance, claimTitn, withdrawRemainingTitn, quoteTitn, setLockedStatus, setLaunchTime, getters. None implement a reverse exchange. The TwoWay enum value at IMerge.sol:7 has zero functional impact anywhere.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Deployment uses setLockedStatus(1) which is OneWay, not TwoWay
config_validation.deployment_evidence: arbitrumSetup.ts line: `await mergeTgt.setLockedStatus(1)` confirms OneWay is used in deployment. TwoWay (value 2) is never used in deployment scripts, further confirming it is dead code.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is plausible but low-impact: if the owner sets lockedStatus to TwoWay (value 2), users reading the contract or events might expect bidirectional exchange capability. They deposit TGT expecting to be able to reverse the exchange later, but no reverse function exists. Their TGT is permanently deposited (only owner can withdraw via withdraw()). However, since the deployment script uses OneWay and there's no evidence users are told TwoWay enables reversal, the practical exploitation risk is low. The core defect remains: the code defines semantic states it doesn't implement.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Low-medium severity. The TwoWay enum value constitutes dead code with misleading semantics. If governance or an owner sets TwoWay expecting different behavior, they get none. Users cannot reverse deposits regardless of mode. The owner can withdraw TGT via the withdraw() function, but users have no self-service reversal. This is a code quality issue (dead code / incomplete implementation) rather than a critical vulnerability, but it meets the definition of a code defect since the enum defines a semantic contract that the implementation does not fulfill.
recommended_mitigations: Remove the TwoWay enum value if reverse exchange is not planned, reducing the enum to just Locked and OneWay (or Locked and Unlocked for clarity)
If TwoWay functionality is intended, implement a reverseTitnToTgt() function gated by lockedStatus == LockedStatus.TwoWay
Add NatSpec documentation to the LockedStatus enum clarifying the intended meaning of each value
Consider using a simple boolean (locked/unlocked) if only two functional states are needed
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy MergeTgt with valid TGT and TITN addresses. 2. Call setLockedStatus(LockedStatus.TwoWay). 3. Verify onTokenTransfer accepts deposits identically to OneWay mode. 4. Attempt to find any function that reverses a deposit (returns TGT for TITN) - confirm none exists. 5. Confirm TwoWay and OneWay produce identical state transitions via event comparison.
uncertainties: Whether TwoWay was intentionally left as a placeholder for future upgrades or is genuinely missing functionality
Whether any off-chain documentation or UI references TwoWay as enabling reverse exchanges
Whether the team planned to implement reverse exchange in a future contract upgrade (though no upgrade mechanism exists)
additional_testing_needed: Review any off-chain documentation or UI that references LockedStatus values to assess user-facing impact
Confirm no proxy or upgrade mechanism could add TwoWay functionality later (current contracts are non-upgradeable)
Test that setting TwoWay produces identical gas costs and state changes as OneWay to fully confirm functional equivalence
